content.text: Feature Flags During GenAI Feature Development

Feature flags let you merge GenAI feature code to the main branch before it is ready for all users, then progressively expose it without a code deploy. For GenAI features this is especially valuable: a new agent behaviour or prompt redesign can be iterated in production — with real traffic, real edge cases, and real latency — while the legacy path remains available as an instant fallback.

This sub-subtopic covers how to design flag schemas that are specific to AI features (pinning model version and prompt version alongside the rollout percentage), how to structure the flag lifecycle from development through flag retirement, and how to implement the kill switch that makes every stage rollback-safe.

attrs.alt: Feature flag lifecycle for GenAI features: Dev → Internal → Beta → GA → Retired

content.text: Why Standard Feature Flags Are Insufficient for AI Features
Standard feature flags track one bit:
enabled: true/false
. For GenAI features, the flag needs to carry additional AI-specific state:
Standard Flag Field
GenAI-Specific Addition
Why It Matters
enabled
kill_switch
Separate kill-switch allows instant OFF without clearing other fields; easier to re-enable after investigation
rollout_pct
allowlist
Allow specific users (internal team, beta testers) regardless of rollout percentage
(none)
model
Lock the flag to a specific model version; model upgrades require a new flag version, not a code change
(none)
prompt_version
Reference the prompt registry version in use; enables auditability of which prompt was live at any time
(none)
eval_baseline_id
Record which eval baseline was used to gate this flag promotion; links incident to specific quality snapshot
Flag Schema and Data Model

# flags/schema.py
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class AIFeatureFlag:
    """
    Schema for a feature flag governing an AI feature.
    Store in Redis, DynamoDB, LaunchDarkly custom attributes, or a JSON config.
    """
    name: str
    enabled: bool
    rollout_pct: float              # 0.0–100.0 — % of all users who see the AI feature
    allowlist: list[str]            # user IDs or group slugs always enabled regardless of pct
    blocklist: list[str]            # user IDs or group slugs always disabled regardless of pct
    model: str                      # model ID pinned to this flag version
    prompt_version: str             # version tag from the prompt registry (e.g. "v2.3.1")
    eval_baseline_id: str           # ID of the eval baseline that gated this promotion
    kill_switch: bool = False       # instant global OFF; overrides all other fields if True
    metadata: dict = field(default_factory=dict)

    def is_enabled_for(self, user_id: str, user_groups: list[str] = None) -> bool:
        """Return True if this flag is active for the given user."""
        if self.kill_switch:
            return False
        if not self.enabled:
            return False
        user_groups = user_groups or []
        # Blocklist wins over allowlist
        if user_id in self.blocklist:
            return False
        if any(g in self.blocklist for g in user_groups):
            return False
        # Allowlist bypasses rollout_pct
        if user_id in self.allowlist:
            return True
        if any(g in self.allowlist for g in user_groups):
            return True
        # Percentage rollout (deterministic by user_id hash)
        import hashlib
        bucket = int(hashlib.md5(f"{self.name}:{user_id}".encode()).hexdigest(), 16) % 100
        return bucket < self.rollout_pct
Loading and Caching Flags at Runtime

Flags must be hot-reloadable without a service restart — that is what enables the kill switch to take effect within seconds. The flag reader caches flags with a short TTL (30s) and re-fetches on expiry, so a kill-switch activation propagates to all instances within one TTL window.

# flags/registry.py
import json, time
from threading import Lock
from flags.schema import AIFeatureFlag

class FlagRegistry:
    """
    Thread-safe flag registry with short-TTL cache for hot-reloading.
    In production, back this with Redis, DynamoDB, or a managed flag service.
    """

    def __init__(self, store, ttl_seconds: float = 30.0):
        self._store = store          # any object with .get(name) -> dict | None
        self._ttl = ttl_seconds
        self._cache: dict[str, tuple[float, AIFeatureFlag | None]] = {}
        self._lock = Lock()

    def get(self, name: str) -> AIFeatureFlag | None:
        with self._lock:
            now = time.monotonic()
            if name in self._cache:
                fetched_at, flag = self._cache[name]
                if now - fetched_at < self._ttl:
                    return flag
            # Cache miss or expired — re-fetch
            raw = self._store.get(name)
            flag = AIFeatureFlag(**raw) if raw else None
            self._cache[name] = (now, flag)
            return flag

    def is_enabled_for(self, name: str, user_id: str, user_groups: list[str] = None) -> bool:
        flag = self.get(name)
        if flag is None:
            return False    # unknown flag = disabled
        return flag.is_enabled_for(user_id, user_groups)


# Usage in a request handler:
# registry = FlagRegistry(store=RedisStore())
# if registry.is_enabled_for("ai_summariser_v2", user_id=request.user_id):
#     response = await ai_summariser.handle(request)
# else:
#     response = await legacy_summariser.handle(request)
The Development Workflow with Flags

Using feature flags during the build phase enables a workflow where the team merges code to main continuously — avoiding long-lived feature branches — while keeping in-progress AI features invisible to users until they are ready.
Create the flag as OFF
before writing any feature code. This decouples deployment from release — code can be merged and deployed without any user impact.
Develop with the flag ON locally
using an environment variable override:
AI_SUMMARISER_V2=true
. No code change needed to test the feature in development.
Enable for the internal team
by adding team member IDs to
allowlist
. The rollout_pct stays at 0; team members see the feature, all other users see legacy.
Run the eval gate
to establish a baseline before any user sees the feature. Gate the internal → beta promotion on eval scores passing.
Increment rollout_pct
from 0 → 5 → 25 → 50 → 100 over days or weeks, monitoring error rate, latency, and eval scores at each step.
Retire the flag
once the feature is at 100% and stable. Remove the flag check from code in a follow-up PR — this is
flag retirement
, and it hardens the feature into the codebase without a bypass path.
Flag Retirement: Hardening the Feature

A feature flag that stays in the codebase indefinitely is technical debt. Every flag check is a branch that must be maintained, tested, and explained to new engineers. Flag retirement is the step that removes the branch and makes the AI behaviour the unconditional default.

Schedule flag retirement as a follow-up PR within one sprint of reaching 100% rollout. Every week a flag stays past its retirement date is a week of unnecessary code complexity and a week where a misconfigured kill switch could silently revert users to the legacy path.
Anti-Patterns in GenAI Feature Flagging
Sharing flags between AI and non-AI features
— a flag that controls both a UI change and a prompt change mixes deployment and release concerns. Each AI feature should have its own flag with AI-specific fields.
Using global flags without per-user bucketing
— a flag that is simply
ON
or
OFF
globally cannot do a gradual rollout. Always use deterministic per-user bucketing so the same user consistently sees the same experience.
Not pinning model version in the flag

— if the flag does not specify which model version is active, a model upgrade can change behaviour for users in the rollout without changing the flag — making the model upgrade and the rollout indistinguishable in incident analysis.
Never retiring flags
— flags that live indefinitely accumulate. Each one is a potential source of confusion and a bypass path that may be accidentally activated. Treat flag retirement as a first-class engineering task.
Kill switch that requires a code deploy
— a kill switch only works if it can be activated within seconds. If activating it requires a code change, a PR, and a deploy pipeline, it is not a kill switch — it is just another deployment.
Testing Flag-Gated Code

Flag-gated code has two execution paths. Both must be tested: the AI path (flag ON) and the legacy path (flag OFF). Leaving the legacy path untested means a regression may appear when you activate the kill switch during an incident — the worst possible time to discover that the fallback is broken.

# tests/test_summariser.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, patch
from flags.schema import AIFeatureFlag
from handlers.summarise import SummariseHandler

def make_flag(enabled: bool, rollout_pct: float = 100.0, kill_switch: bool = False):
    return AIFeatureFlag(
        name="ai_summariser_v2",
        enabled=enabled,
        rollout_pct=rollout_pct,
        allowlist=[],
        blocklist=[],
        model="claude-sonnet-5",
        prompt_version="v2.3.1",
        eval_baseline_id="baseline-2026-07-01",
        kill_switch=kill_switch,
    )

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_flag_on_uses_ai_handler():
    flag = make_flag(enabled=True)
    ai_handler = AsyncMock(return_value="AI summary")
    legacy_handler = AsyncMock(return_value="Legacy summary")
    handler = SummariseHandler(flag=flag, ai=ai_handler, legacy=legacy_handler)

    result = await handler.summarise(text="long text", user_id="u1")
    assert result == "AI summary"
    ai_handler.assert_called_once()
    legacy_handler.assert_not_called()

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_flag_off_uses_legacy_handler():
    flag = make_flag(enabled=False)
    ai_handler = AsyncMock(return_value="AI summary")
    legacy_handler = AsyncMock(return_value="Legacy summary")
    handler = SummariseHandler(flag=flag, ai=ai_handler, legacy=legacy_handler)

    result = await handler.summarise(text="long text", user_id="u1")
    assert result == "Legacy summary"
    ai_handler.assert_not_called()

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_kill_switch_forces_legacy():
    flag = make_flag(enabled=True, rollout_pct=100.0, kill_switch=True)
    ai_handler = AsyncMock(return_value="AI summary")
    legacy_handler = AsyncMock(return_value="Legacy summary")
    handler = SummariseHandler(flag=flag, ai=ai_handler, legacy=legacy_handler)

    result = await handler.summarise(text="long text", user_id="u1")
    assert result == "Legacy summary"
    ai_handler.assert_not_called()

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_allowlist_user_bypasses_zero_pct():
    flag = AIFeatureFlag(
        name="ai_summariser_v2", enabled=True, rollout_pct=0.0,
        allowlist=["internal_user_123"], blocklist=[],
        model="claude-sonnet-5", prompt_version="v2.3.1",
        eval_baseline_id="b1",
    )
    ai_handler = AsyncMock(return_value="AI summary")
    legacy_handler = AsyncMock(return_value="Legacy summary")
    handler = SummariseHandler(flag=flag, ai=ai_handler, legacy=legacy_handler)

    result = await handler.summarise(text="long text", user_id="internal_user_123")
    assert result == "AI summary"
Flag Observability: Tracing Which Path Was Taken

When debugging a production incident, knowing whether a specific request was served by the AI path or the legacy path is critical. Emit this as a structured trace field on every request so that it appears in your logging and monitoring dashboards alongside latency, error rate, and model outputs.

# handlers/summarise.py — with observability
import structlog
from flags.schema import AIFeatureFlag

log = structlog.get_logger()

class SummariseHandler:
    def __init__(self, flag: AIFeatureFlag, ai, legacy):
        self._flag = flag
        self._ai = ai
        self._legacy = legacy

    async def summarise(self, text: str, user_id: str, trace_id: str = "") -> str:
        use_ai = self._flag.is_enabled_for(user_id)
        handler_name = "ai_v2" if use_ai else "legacy"
        handler = self._ai if use_ai else self._legacy

        log.info("summarise_request",
            trace_id=trace_id,
            user_id=user_id,
            flag=self._flag.name,
            handler=handler_name,
            model=self._flag.model if use_ai else None,
            prompt_version=self._flag.prompt_version if use_ai else None,
        )

        result = await handler(text)
        return result
The fields
flag
handler
model
, and
prompt_version
should all be present in your trace span. This allows you to filter your monitoring dashboards to
show only requests that went through the AI path
and compare their latency and error rate against legacy-path requests in the same time window.
Coordinating Flag State Across Microservices

When a GenAI feature spans multiple microservices — for example, the prompt service, the tool gateway, and the agent orchestrator all need to behave consistently for a given user — all services must read from the same flag store and apply the same bucketing logic.
Coordination Pattern
Mechanism
Trade-off
Centralised flag store
All services read the same Redis/DynamoDB instance with 30s TTL cache
Single source of truth; propagation lag ≤ TTL
Flag value in request header
API gateway evaluates the flag once and sets X-AI-Feature-Enabled; downstream services trust the header
Fast; requires trusting internal network; harder to audit per-service
Flag value in JWT
Auth token includes flag decisions at login time
Consistent within a session; stale for sessions that outlive the rollout change

The centralised flag store pattern is the most robust for GenAI feature rollouts because kill-switch activations propagate to all services within one TTL window without requiring a re-login or a header change. The tradeoff is that each service makes an independent flag evaluation call — but with a 30s local cache, this is negligible overhead.
Connecting Flags to the CI Pipeline
The flag schema pins
prompt_version
and
eval_baseline_id
— but only if these values are actually kept in sync with what is deployed. Automate the linkage by having CI update the flag config when a new prompt version passes its eval gate:

# .github/workflows/promote-prompt-version.yml
# After eval gate passes on a prompt PR, update the flag's prompt_version.
name: Promote Prompt Version to Flag

on:
  workflow_run:
    workflows: ["Eval Regression Gate"]
    types: [completed]

jobs:
  promote:
    if: ${{ github.event.workflow_run.conclusion == 'success' }}
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Extract new prompt version
        id: version
        run: |
          VERSION=$(cat prompts/VERSION)
          echo "version=$VERSION" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - name: Update flag config in Redis
        run: |
          python3 scripts/update_flag_prompt_version.py \
            --flag ai_summariser_v2 \
            --prompt-version ${{ steps.version.outputs.version }} \
            --eval-baseline-id $(cat evals/baselines/LATEST_ID)
        env:
          REDIS_URL: ${{ secrets.REDIS_URL }}

This closes the loop: a prompt change triggers an eval run, the eval run gates promotion, and on success the flag config is updated to record which prompt version and baseline are now live. The flag config becomes a real-time audit log of AI feature state, not just a rollout switch.
Summary

Feature flags during GenAI development serve a dual purpose: they enable trunk-based development by decoupling code merges from feature releases, and they provide a kill switch that makes every stage of a GenAI rollout instantly reversible. The AI-specific flag schema — pinning model version, prompt version, and eval baseline alongside the rollout percentage — creates an audit trail that links every production AI behaviour to the exact code, model, and prompt state that produced it.

The lifecycle discipline — develop OFF, internal allowlist, eval-gated beta, gradual percentage rollout, flag retirement — turns a risky big-bang AI feature launch into a series of low-risk, measurable steps where each transition is gated by observable quality metrics.
